sentences.base_language: On my way to work I stopped at the bakery.
sentences.grammarNote:
**V-는 길에** (neun gire) = 'on the way to do / while on the way': 출근하는 길에 = 'on the way to work.'
**들르다** (deulleurda) = 'to stop by / drop in' — an irregular ㄹ verb: 들러요 (present), 들렀어요 (past).
**빵집** (ppangjip) = bakery — 빵 (bread, from Portuguese 'pão') + 집 (house/shop). Very common colloquial term alongside the loanword 베이커리 (beikori).

sentences.base_language: The smell of fresh bread was wonderful.
sentences.target_language: 갓 구운 빵 냄새가 정말 좋았어요.
sentences.romanized: Gat guun ppang naemsaega jeongmal joasseoyo.
sentences.literal: Just baked bread smell really good-was.
sentences.grammarNote:
**갓** (gat) = 'just / freshly' — an adverb meaning something was done very recently: 갓 구운 (just baked), 갓 만든 (just made).
**구운** is the past-modifier form of 굽다 (to bake/grill): 구운 빵 = 'baked bread.'
**냄새** (naemsae) = smell/scent. 냄새가 나다 = 'a smell comes (from)'. 냄새가 좋다 = 'the smell is good.' Korean often uses **가/이 나다** to express a sense: 소리가 나다 (a sound comes), 맛이 나다 (a taste comes).

sentences.base_language: Good morning! What would you like today?
sentences.grammarNote:
**드릴까요?** (deurillkkayo?) = 'shall I give you?' — **드리다** is the humble/honorific form of 주다 (to give), used when the giver is lower status than the receiver.
The **-(으)ㄹ까요?** ending asks for the listener's preference or proposes an action: 뭘 드릴까요? = 'what shall I give you? / what would you like?'
**뭘** is a contracted form of 무엇을 (what + object marker). Contractions are very common in everyday spoken Korean.

sentences.base_language: I would like one sourdough loaf, please.
sentences.grammarNote: -주세요 (juseyo) is the standard polite request form: 'please give me.' It comes from 주다 (to give) in the honorific imperative. For actions you want someone to do for you, use -아/어 주세요: 포장해 주세요 (please wrap it), 잘라 주세요 (please slice it). The -주세요 ending is one of the most useful request patterns.

sentences.base_language: And also one of those sweet red bean pastries.
sentences.grammarNote: 하나, 둘, 셋 are the native Korean numbers used for counting objects with counters. 하나 (one) becomes 한 before a counter: 빵 한 개 (one piece of bread), 커피 한 잔 (one cup of coffee). 둘 (two) becomes 두: 빵 두 개 (two pieces of bread). Always use native Korean numbers for counting things, not Sino-Korean.

sentences.base_language: Great choice! The red bean pastries just came out of the oven.
sentences.grammarNote:
**선택이세요** (seontaegisoyo) = 'it is your choice' — the honorific copula **이세요** (isoyo) respectfully describes the customer's action.
**방금** (banggeum) = 'just now / a moment ago' — used for something that happened very recently.
**나오다** (naoda) = 'to come out' — a directional compound of 나 (out) + 오다 (come). 나왔어요 = 'just came out.'
**단팥빵** (danpatppang) = sweet red bean bread — 단 (sweet) + 팥 (red bean) + 빵 (bread). A classic Korean bakery staple.

sentences.base_language: Can I also get one iced coffee?
sentences.target_language: 아이스 커피도 하나 주실 수 있어요?
sentences.romanized: Aiseu keopido hana jusil su isseoyo?
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄹ 수 있어요?** (-(eu)l su isseoyo?) = 'can you...? / is it possible to...?' — a polite way to make a request or ask about ability.
**주실 수 있어요?** uses the honorific stem 주시다 (to give, respectfully): 'could you please give?'
**도** (do) = 'also/too' after 커피 — requesting an additional item: 커피도 (coffee too).
아이스 커피 is the standard Korean term for iced/cold coffee, borrowed from English.

sentences.base_language: Of course. Will you be eating here or taking it to go?
sentences.target_language: 물론이죠. 여기서 드실 건가요, 포장해 드릴까요?
sentences.romanized: Mullonjyo. Yeogiseo deusil geongayo, pojanghae deurillkkayo?
sentences.literal: Of-course. Here eat will, wrap shall-I-give?
sentences.grammarNote: 포장해 드릴까요? (pojanghae deurillkkayo?) = 'Shall I wrap it for you?' This is a set phrase you will hear at every Korean bakery and café. 드릴까요 is the honorific of 줄까요 (shall I give?), used to show respect to the customer. The 드리다 form is always used when giving something to someone of higher status.

sentences.base_language: To go, please. I'm in a hurry.
sentences.grammarNote:
**포장해 주세요** (pojanghae juseyo) = 'please wrap it / to go please' — 포장하다 (to wrap/pack) + 해 주세요 (please do for me).
**좀** (jom) = 'a bit / somewhat' — also softens requests: 좀 주세요 (please give me a little).
**급해서요** ends with **-아서/어서요**, a polite sentence-final form that gives a reason: 급해서요 = 'because I'm in a hurry (you see).' The trailing 요 adds politeness without completing a full clause.

sentences.base_language: That will be eight thousand five hundred won.
sentences.grammarNote:
Korean prices use Sino-Korean numbers: 팔 (8) + 천 (1,000) + 오 (5) + 백 (100) = 8,500.
**원입니다** (wonipnida) is the formal polite statement — 원 (won) + 입니다 (formal copula 'is').
Korean won (₩) has no coins of value in daily use — the smallest practical unit is 100원.
Announcers and shop staff often use the formal **-ㅂ니다/습니다** speech level rather than casual **-아요/어요** when stating prices.

sentences.base_language: Here you go. Have a nice day!
sentences.grammarNote:
**여기 있어요** (yeogi isseoyo) = 'here it is / here you go' — literally 'it exists here'. Used when handing something over.
**좋은 하루 보내세요** (joeun haru bonaeseyo) is the standard farewell meaning 'have a nice day' — literally 'please spend a good day.'
**보내다** (bonaeda) = 'to send' or 'to spend (time)'. 하루를 보내다 = 'to spend a day.'
**-세요** is the polite imperative/suggestion form: 보내세요 = 'please (have / spend).

sentences.base_language: Thank you! The bread smells amazing.
sentences.romanized: Gamsahaeyo! Ppang naemsaega neomu jonne yo.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-네요** (neyo) is a sentence-final ending expressing a fresh discovery or mild surprise: 좋네요 = 'oh, it's really good!' or 'how nice!'
It implies the speaker is noticing or realising something, adding a sense of spontaneous reaction.
Contrast: 좋아요 (it's good — neutral statement) vs. 좋네요 (oh, it's good! — expressing a feeling of discovery).
**냄새가 나다** is the standard phrase 'to smell (of something)'; 냄새가 좋다 means the smell is pleasant.
